text: Trocar placement is visible with one positioned at the umbilicus using the open Hassan technique, two in the left flank, and a fourth in the epigastrium. Minimal evacuation of the perisplenic blood clot is performed to prevent sudden arterial bleeding.

The surgeon uses a suction tube to bluntly suspend the spleen, minimizing parenchymal trauma and capsular tears. The spleen is rapidly freed from its inferior, posterior, and lateral attachments using a hook, detaching it from the splenocolic, splenophrenic, and splenorenal ligaments to achieve sufficient mobilization and medial rotation.
The surgeon exposes the splenic hilum and controls it by clamping with a flexible endostapler, securing the area to prevent bleeding during the splenectomy.

The surgeon completes the splenectomy by gradually stapling and dividing the splenic hilar vessels and short gastric vessels using a flexible endostapler. The splenic hilar vessels are stapled and divided, followed by the short gastric vessels with a separate load.

The surgeon carefully explores the left diaphragm, splenic-colonic flexure, and gastric greater curvature, confirming the absence of associated injuries. After irrigating the area and verifying clear lavage fluid, topical hemostatic agents are applied to the splenic bed.

Corrugated drains are inserted in the left upper quadrant and Douglas pouch. The entire spleen is placed into a large endo bag, and the splenic parenchyma is extracted through the umbilical port following complete morcellation.
